title: Introduction
content:
Source Code: Document-Management-System
What is the DMS Project? The Document Management System (DMS) is the final project of the internship program, applying everything learned about AWS Serverless into a single, cohesive, production-grade system.
The system addresses real-world enterprise pain points:
Secure, centralized document storage with version history Role-based access control for upload, view, and share operations Asynchronous upload pipeline that handles large files without bottlenecks Immutable audit trail for all document operations Automatic malware scanning on every uploaded file Test Accounts & Access Website URL: https://dmt3lkrot0e6x.cloudfront.net/
Role Username Password System Admin admin@gmail.com Admin12345@ Employee fin123@gmail.com Test12345.@A Department Admin depart123@gmail.com Test12345.@A Note: This is an internal website, so the UI will not have a self-registration option. Accounts must be granted by the Admin.
System Architecture Diagram AWS Services Used Service Role in the System Amazon Cognito User pool, JWT auth, 3 user groups (EMPLOYEE / DEPT_ADMIN / SYS_ADMIN) API Gateway REST API, Cognito Authorizer, per-endpoint IAM authorization AWS Lambda 12 serverless handlers, Node.js 22, ARM64, X-Ray tracing Amazon S3 QuarantineBucket (upload staging), DocumentsBucket (final storage) Amazon DynamoDB Single-table design, 4 GSIs, PAY_PER_REQUEST billing, PITR enabled Amazon EventBridge Event bus routing S3 events to processing Lambda functions GuardDuty Malware Protection Plan scanning all quarantine uploads Amazon CloudFront CDN for React SPA frontend served from S3 AWS CDK Full IaC deployment in TypeScript CloudWatch + SNS Log retention, metric alarms, email notifications Key Design Decisions Presigned URL for uploads: Files never pass through API Gateway (avoids 10MB payload limit). The frontend gets a presigned PUT URL and uploads directly to S3 QuarantineBucket. Quarantine-first upload: All files are held in QuarantineBucket until GuardDuty clears them. Only CLEAN-tagged files are moved to the production DocumentsBucket. Single-table DynamoDB: All entities (Document, Version, Share, AuditLog, UploadIntent, UserProfile) share one table with composite keys for cost efficiency. Immutable audit log: AuditLog records are append-only, written with attribute_not_exists conditional PutItem to prevent tampering. Least Privilege IAM: Each Lambda function has its own IAM role with only the permissions it needs — no shared service roles.

title: Event 1
content:
Summary Report: FCAJ Community Day (May 23) Purpose of the Event The FCAJ Community Day (May 23) event was organized to create a space for sharing knowledge, introducing practical projects, and updating new technology trends within the FCAJ community. The main objectives of the event included:
Providing opportunities for members to present technology projects, ideas, and solutions they have researched or deployed. Sharing knowledge regarding Generative AI, Large Language Models, Multi-Agent Systems, and their application directions in enterprise environments. Introducing solutions for optimizing performance and security of web infrastructures through Amazon CloudFront. Spreading real-world experience from Hackathon competitions, particularly how to develop products within a short timeframe. Helping attendees expand their perspectives on how to combine technology, product mindset, and teamwork skills. List of Speakers & Topics Tinh - Topic: Build Second Brain Hai Anh - Topic: Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick Thinh Nguyen - Topic: From Edge To Origin: CloudFront as Your Foundation Team VIB - Topic: 36 hrs with LotusHacks – Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality Dao Duc - Topic: Deep dive talk: How LLM actually works? Cat Vy - Topic: Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System: The Case of Startup Credit Scoring Key Highlights of the Presentations 1. Build Second Brain - Speaker: Tinh The presentation focused on methods for building a personal knowledge management system. Instead of fragmented note-taking, learners can organize information in a connected, easy-to-retrieve, and reusable manner. This content helped me understand that learning technology depends not only on the amount of knowledge received, but also heavily on how that knowledge is stored, systematized, and exploited when needed.
2. Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick - Speaker: Hai Anh The presentation introduced a direction for building a friendly AI assistant to support users in interacting with and exploiting data more visually. The content emphasized how to apply AWS services to create chatbots capable of analyzing and responding according to user needs. Through this part, I gained more perspective on how AI applications do not just stop at answering questions, but can also combine with data to support better decision-making.
3. From Edge To Origin: CloudFront as Your Foundation - Speaker: Thinh Nguyen The presentation explained the role of Amazon CloudFront in distributing content from Edge Locations to Origin Servers. The content focused on how CloudFront helps reduce latency, accelerate resource loading speeds, and contribute to safeguarding backend systems. This is a highly practical part because CloudFront is a service commonly encountered in modern web architectures, especially when deploying frontends, static websites, or systems with users distributed across multiple regions.
4. 36 hrs with LotusHacks – Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality - Team VIB Team VIB shared the process of developing the UTMorpho product within a 36-hour Hackathon. The content covered not only technical aspects but also mentioned how the team brainstormed ideas, assigned roles, handled errors, and finalized the demo in a very short time. This part clearly showed the pressure of a real competitive environment, while helping me better understand the importance of prioritizing core features, team coordination, and quick decision-making.
5. How LLM actually works? - Speaker: Dao Duc The presentation went deep into how large language models operate. Concepts such as Transformers, Self-Attention, Pre-training, and Fine-tuning were presented to help attendees better understand the technical foundation behind LLMs. After this part, I understood that an LLM is not just a text generation tool, but the result of multiple layers of data processing, model training, and fine-tuning to fit specific problems.
6. Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System - Speaker: Cat Vy The presentation introduced how to build an enterprise-grade Multi-Agent system. Agents can coordinate with each other to handle complex tasks, such as financial analysis or credit scoring for startups. This content helped me better visualize how AI can be organized into multiple specialized components, instead of just using a single model to process the entire workload.
Key Takeaways Regarding Generative AI & New Technologies Gained more understanding of the technical foundation of LLMs, particularly Transformers and Self-Attention. Learned the differences between the initial pre-training phase and the model fine-tuning phase. Acquired further knowledge on how to design Multi-Agent systems to serve business problems. Recognized that AI can be applied in various fields such as chatbots, financial analysis, process automation, and user support. Regarding Cloud Infrastructure Architecture Better understood the role of Amazon CloudFront in optimizing application access speeds. Learned how CDNs help reduce load on Origin Servers and improve user experiences. Acquired more direction when designing web systems that require faster, more stable, and more secure content delivery. Recognized that choosing the appropriate infrastructure architecture directly affects operational performance and costs. Regarding Soft Skills & Working Methodologies Learned how to organize personal knowledge using the Second Brain method. Understood the importance of systematic note-taking when learning new technologies. Gathered experiences on how to work in teams under high-pressure environments. Realized that when developing products in a short time, it is necessary to prioritize the MVP and focus on core values. Professional Applications Personal knowledge management: Apply the Second Brain method to store notes, AWS documents, workshops, and lessons learned during the internship process. Web infrastructure optimization: Research further on how to use Amazon CloudFront for static websites or frontend applications deployed on AWS. Exploring AI Agents: Continue testing simple chatbot models or agents to support repetitive tasks in studying and working. Rapid product development: Apply lessons from the Hackathon by focusing on important functionalities first, then expanding and finalizing gradually. Improving teamwork skills: Pay more attention to task division, time management, and clear communication during project execution. Overall Event Experience The event brought me many practical experiences because the contents did not just stop at theory but also included demos, case studies, and sharing from the speakers’ actual deployment processes. The atmosphere of the sharing session was quite open, and the topics were presented from many different angles: from personal knowledge management, AI assistants, CDNs, Hackathons, to LLMs and Multi-Agent Systems. This helped me get a broader view of how technology is applied in reality. I was particularly impressed with the sharing sections about LLMs, CloudFront, and Hackathons, as these contents can be directly related to the process of learning AWS, doing workshops, and building personal projects.
Lessons Learned Learning technology needs to go along with methods for storing and systematizing knowledge. When building a system, it is crucial to clearly understand the real-world problem before choosing the technology. AI and Cloud are increasingly tightly integrated in modern solutions. Effective teamwork requires clear assignment, continuous communication, and quick adaptability. A good product does not necessarily have to be perfect from the start, but needs to have a clear development direction starting from an MVP version.

title: Blog 1
content:
HOW TO BUILD A COST-EFFECTIVE AWS KMS STRATEGY FOR MULTI-TENANT APPLICATIONS While exploring AWS architectures for SaaS systems, I realized that data encryption in a multi-tenant environment is not just a security concern, but is also directly tied to cost and long-term operational efficiency. If every tenant or every service creates its own standalone KMS key, the system can quickly become overwhelmed with key management issues, escalating costs, and audit complexities. Therefore, AWS proposes a more optimized approach: centralizing key management by combining AWS KMS, IAM Roles, AWS STS, and Aliases to ensure robust security while keeping costs highly efficient.
Key Points to Master: Centralizing Key Management in a Dedicated Account Customer-managed keys for each tenant are managed centrally in a specialized security account. This approach streamlines key lifecycle management, making creation, rotation, deletion, and auditing much easier. It avoids key fragmentation across multiple accounts or disconnected services. Leveraging Aliases to Identify Keys per Tenant Each tenant can be assigned a dedicated alias, for example: alias/customer-<tenant-id>. Services do not need to hardcode direct Key IDs. When a key needs to be changed or rotated, the system can update the alias mapping without impacting application logic. Utilizing IAM Roles and AWS STS for Temporary Access Delegation Services running in the customer-facing account can assume a cross-account role into the key management account. Access permissions are temporary and strictly controlled via scoped IAM Policies. This minimizes the risk of long-term credential leaks and strictly aligns with AWS security best practices. Encrypting Data Prior to Storage Sensitive data such as passwords, API keys, or license data can be encrypted before being persisted into DynamoDB or S3. Each tenant utilizes a unique key, reinforcing data isolation boundaries. Even if multiple tenants share a unified storage system, their data remains securely isolated on a per-tenant basis. Seamless Scalability as Tenant Volume Grows When onboarding a new tenant, the system simply provisions a new key and its corresponding alias. Customer-facing services require no code modifications. This architectural pattern effectively prevents “key explosion” as the platform expands. Operational Workflow in Practice: The end-to-end data processing flow can be mapped as follows:
The client transmits sensitive data into the system. The service receives the request and extracts tenant metadata from the incoming JWT. The Lambda function validates the JWT and determines the structural tenant ID. The service performs an assume-role operation to access the central key management account. The system uses the targeted alias format alias/customer-<tenant-id> to locate the exact KMS key for that tenant. The payload is encrypted using AWS KMS or the AWS Encryption SDK. The encrypted ciphertext payload is securely stored within Amazon DynamoDB or Amazon S3 targets. The Role of Deployed AWS Services: AWS KMS: Manages customer-managed keys used to drive data encryption operations. IAM & AWS STS: Grants temporary cross-account access capabilities using structured assume-role execution patterns. AWS Lambda: Intercepts ingress requests, validates JWT parameters, and executes the core encryption processing logic. Amazon DynamoDB / Amazon S3: Retains encrypted data structures at-rest. Aliases: Discovers keys dynamically on a per-tenant basis, eliminating hardcoded Key IDs within application source trees. AWS CloudTrail: Aggregates centralized audit histories tracking key access operations and configuration changes. Delivered Business Value: This centralized AWS KMS key strategy delivers critical optimizations for multi-tenant SaaS environments:
Drastically scales down key management overhead and administrative costs as tenant numbers increase. Facilitates seamless operations, simplified compliance auditing, and precise key lifecycle governance. Enforces absolute logical data isolation boundaries between multi-tenant workloads. Mitigates the risk of overly permissive credential exposure. Facilitates agile platform scaling without demanding adjustments to core application processing layers. Aligns perfectly with enterprise SaaS platforms or corporate data networks managing highly sensitive datasets. Conclusion AWS KMS is a highly performant encryption service, but deploying it within a multi-tenant framework demands a well-architected design strategy to properly balance security, financial expenditure, and operational overhead. By instituting a centralized key management model that integrates IAM Roles, AWS STS, and Aliases, platforms guarantee that each tenant retains rigorous data isolation boundaries while operational costs and complexities remain highly optimized. For cloud architects, this represents an essential design pattern to master when building scalable, secure SaaS applications on AWS that handle highly sensitive information.
Illustrative Diagram Original Blog Reference https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/architecture/simplify-multi-tenant-encryption-with-a-cost-conscious-aws-kms-key-strategy/

title: Proposal
content:
1. Project Summary The Document Management System (DMS) project is built to create a secure, highly scalable, and cost-effective internal document management system on top of the AWS Serverless platform. The system allows users to log in, upload documents, manage versions, share documents based on granular permissions, and track continuous operational histories. DMS utilizes core AWS services including Amazon Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, S3, DynamoDB, EventBridge, GuardDuty, CloudFront, CloudWatch, and SNS.
2. Problem Statement Traditional methods of storing and sharing internal documents often face severe constraints such as difficulties in version control, lack of fine-grained access control, hardships in auditing operational histories, and potential risks of corporate data exposure. The proposed solution is to build a document management system leveraging a 100% Serverless architecture, where raw file streams transfer directly to Amazon S3 via secure Presigned URLs, undergo automated malware screening prior to permanent storage, and have all metadata managed within optimized DynamoDB single-table layouts.
3. Solution Architecture The system operates along the primary workflow:
End-users access the system securely over accelerated Amazon CloudFront edge connections. The static Frontend application is hosted out of an isolated Amazon S3 FrontendBucket. Users authenticate via Amazon Cognito identity directories and receive secure JWT tokens. The Frontend client dispatches API requests through Amazon API Gateway. API Gateway validates the JWT signatures natively using a built-in Cognito Authorizer. Core API Lambdas process primary business logic such as account profiles, document indexes, and file specifications. Intent Lambdas calculate ephemeral Presigned URLs to handle data ingestion and data egress (upload/download paths). The Frontend streams files directly into the secure Amazon S3 QuarantineBucket via the generated upload link. Amazon GuardDuty performs automated binary screening (Malware Protection) on the ingested asset. The central Amazon EventBridge bus intercepts state transitions and triggers the Upload Processor Function. The Upload Processor parses security tag indicators, updates item operational states inside DynamoDB, and safely transfers clean assets into the permanent DocumentsBucket. CloudWatch aggregates execution logging fields and triggers continuous monitoring alerts via SNS if the platform encounters exceptions or anomalies. 4. Technical Implementation Frontend: React SPA, distributed globally via Amazon CloudFront edge networks. Backend: AWS Lambda compute services utilizing Node.js 22 runtime environments to handle business computations. API: Amazon API Gateway REST API interlocked with native Cognito Authorizers. Storage: Amazon S3 multi-bucket layout encompassing FrontendBucket, QuarantineBucket, and DocumentsBucket tiers. Database: Amazon DynamoDB non-relational tables managing document metadata, version history arrays, sharing link metrics, and immutable audit trails. Security: Cognito group claims for fine-grained user authorization, absolute S3 Private Bucket isolations, dynamic short-lived Presigned URLs, strict IAM Least Privilege execution boundaries, and GuardDuty Malware Protection sandboxing. Observability: Centralized CloudWatch Logs auditing, continuous X-Ray tracing diagnostics, and automated SNS Alerts signaling. Infrastructure Provisioning: AWS CDK framework following modern Infrastructure as Code (IaC) deployment models. 5. Deployment Roadmap Week 9: Prerequisite requirements analysis, comprehensive system architectural mapping, and target workspace environment staging. Week 10: Multi-tier core DMS project infrastructure provisioning utilizing automated AWS CDK pipelines. Week 11: Backend parameter configurations, root global Admin identity directory creation, integration API verification, and end-to-end document ingestion upload loops validation. Week 12: Perimeter security configuration controls checks, deep system observability audits, systematic resource de-provisioning cleanup runs, and comprehensive project retrospective sign-off. 6. Budget Estimation Using a serverless architecture, the system operates on a pay-as-you-use model, with costs only incurred based on actual usage. In the test environment, costs are low thanks to AWS’s Free Tier.
AWS Lambda: ~0.00 USD (within the free limit for 1 million requests/month). Amazon S3: ~0.15 USD for external storage and bandwidth. DynamoDB: ~0.00 USD (25GB of free storage). API Gateway & Cognito: Free for small-scale internal user storage. Total estimated budget: Under $1 USD/month for the test environment and approximately under $10 USD/month for a moderately sized real-world environment. 7. Risk Assessment and Contingency Data Exposure Risk: Neutralized by enforcing absolute S3 Private Bucket configurations, global Block Public Access constraints, dynamic Presigned URLs restricted by tight temporal expiration limits, and role-based user token validation mapping via Amazon Cognito directory groups. Malicious File Ingestion Threat: Remedied by routing all untrusted inbound data streams directly into an isolated QuarantineBucket sandbox, backed by real-time automated threat checking via GuardDuty Malware Protection plans. Payload Size Networking Bottlenecks: Resolved by bypassing compute networks during ingestion blocks, streaming binary files directly from client browsers straight to S3 storage boundaries instead of passing multi-megabyte data payloads across API Gateways. Unexpected Cloud Spend Accrual: Mitigated by setting up automated CloudWatch Alarms monitoring system limits, routing urgent notification messages via SNS topics, and systematically triggering multi-environment resource teardown purges upon project evaluation handoffs. 8. Expected Outcomes The capstone project targets the successful implementation of a highly performant, secure internal enterprise document management system complete with robust multi-tenant authorization partitions, asset version tracking capabilities, immutable append-only compliance ledger recording, and automated perimeter data isolation screening. Through the execution of this deployment lifecycle, engineering teams thoroughly synthesize cutting-edge AWS Serverless architectural design patterns against real-world enterprise requirements, gaining expert-level proficiency in modeling, constructing, securing, and maintaining cloud-native, modern software systems.

title: Prerequisites
content:
Before deploying the DMS project, make sure the following tools and accounts are ready.
1. Required Accounts & Access AWS Account with sufficient permissions to create IAM roles, Lambda, S3, DynamoDB, Cognito, CloudFront, EventBridge, GuardDuty, SNS, CloudWatch, and CDK Bootstrap resources. AWS CLI configured with a profile that has the required permissions. 2. Required Tools Tool Version Purpose Node.js >= 20.x Run CDK and Lambda build npm >= 10.x Package management AWS CDK CLI >= 2.x Deploy infrastructure Git Any Clone the repository Install CDK globally if not already installed:
npm install -g aws-cdk Verify versions:
node --version npm --version cdk --version aws --version 3. Bootstrap CDK (First-time only) CDK Bootstrap creates the required S3 bucket and IAM roles in your AWS account for CDK to store deployment artifacts.
cdk bootstrap aws://<ACCOUNT_ID>/<REGION> Example:
cdk bootstrap aws://123456789012/ap-southeast-1 4. Clone the Repository git clone <your-repository-url> cd DMS npm install 5. Configure Environment Variables Copy the example environment file and fill in your values:
cp .env.example .env The .env.example file contains the required variables:
# Alert email for CloudWatch cost/error notifications DMS_ALERT_EMAIL=your-email@example.com 6. Build Lambda Functions Before deploying, compile the TypeScript Lambda code:
cd aws/functions npm install npm run build cd ../.. 7. Verify Project Structure Make sure the project structure looks like this before deploying:
DMS/ ├── aws/ │ ├── functions/ # Lambda handlers (TypeScript) │ │ └── dist/ # ← Must exist after npm run build │ └── infrastructure/ # AWS CDK stack (TypeScript) ├── frontend/ # React + Vite frontend ├── contracts/ # OpenAPI schemas └── docs/ # Data model & decisions

title: Event 2
content:
Summary Report: “FCAJ Community Day (June 6)” Purpose of the Event The FCAJ Community Day (June 6) event was organized to share practical technological knowledge, spanning from containerization, application security, cloud game servers to AI, GraphRAG, and career orientation within the infrastructure domain. The main objectives of the event included:
Helping attendees better understand Docker and how to package applications for consistent deployment across environments. Introducing how to combine AWS WAF with Machine Learning to detect and respond to anomalous attack behaviors. Sharing deployment models for multiplayer game servers on cloud platforms. Providing methods for effective teamwork in real-world project environments. Introducing the GraphRAG approach utilizing AWS Neptune to construct a Graph Knowledge Base. Orienting the career progression path from an IT Helpdesk position to a Senior System Administrator. List of Speakers & Topics Bao Huynh - Topic: Docker - A containerization technology Le Hoang Gia Dai - Topic: Combining AWS WAF with Machine Learning for Cyber Attack Detection Nguyen Quốc Bao - Topic: Multiplayer in the Cloud: Connecting Godot Clients with AWS Truong Phuoc - Topic: Effective Teamwork Methods Viet Phat - Topic: AWS Neptune for Building a Graph Knowledge Base for GraphRAG Vinh Tran - Topic: From IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin: A Journey of Self-Learning and Transition Roadmap Key Highlights of the Presentations 1. Docker - A containerization technology - Speaker: Bao Huynh The presentation helped attendees clearly understand the differences between virtual machines and containers. While a virtual machine requires a separate operating system for each environment, a container shares the operating system kernel, making it lighter, faster to boot up, and easier to deploy. The speaker also presented core components of Docker such as the Docker Engine, Dockerfile, Docker Image, and Docker Container. Additionally, the Docker Compose section helped me understand how to run multiple services like frontends, backends, and databases within the same configuration environment. This content is highly practical because Docker minimizes environment discrepancy errors between local machines, test environments, and production.
2. Combining AWS WAF with Machine Learning for Cyber Attack Detection - Speaker: Le Hoang Gia Dai The presentation focused on web application security challenges. The speaker pointed out that traditional WAF systems relying on fixed rules could struggle when facing new attack vectors or continuously changing anomalous behaviors. The introduced solution is combining AWS WAF, Kinesis Data Firehose, Amazon S3, AWS Lambda, and Machine Learning models to analyze access logs. Upon detecting anomalies, the system can automatically update the IP blocklist on AWS WAF. Through this topic, I learned more about building proactive security systems instead of merely reacting manually after incidents occur.
3. Multiplayer in the Cloud: Connecting Godot Clients with AWS - Speaker: Nguyen Quoc Bao The presentation introduced how to deploy multiplayer games on the cloud. The content focused on critical challenges such as latency, state synchronization between players, and maintaining real-time connectivity between game clients and servers. The speaker presented how to utilize the Godot Engine to connect with servers via protocols like UDP, TCP, or WebSockets. Concurrently, the talk touched upon deploying dedicated game servers using AWS services like Amazon GameLift, Amazon ECS, or AWS App Runner. The demo portion helped me better visualize how an action from a client is sent to the server and synchronized back to other clients.
4. Effective Teamwork Methods - Speaker: Truong Phuoc This content focused on factors that help a team work more effectively, including clear common goals, transparent communication, trust, and appropriate division of responsibilities. The speaker shared how to apply Agile/Scrum in projects, including activities like Daily Standups, Sprint Planning, and Retrospectives. Furthermore, tools like Jira, Trello, Slack, Discord, Git, and GitHub were mentioned as crucial supporting utilities in the team coordination process. My takeaway is that teamwork is not just about breaking down tasks, but also about communicating properly, providing constructive feedback, and resolving mâu thuẫn in a positive manner.
5. AWS Neptune for Building a Graph Knowledge Base for GraphRAG - Speaker: Viet Phat The presentation introduced the limitations of traditional RAG when relying solely on vector databases. For queries requiring multi-step reasoning or containing deep relationships between entities, traditional RAG might not be effective enough. The GraphRAG solution combines semantic search with graph databases. AWS Neptune is utilized to store entities and relationships in the form of a Graph Knowledge Base. Through query languages like Gremlin or openCypher, the system can retrieve a richer context. This content helped me realize that organizing knowledge properly can significantly improve the answer quality of AI systems.
6. From IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin - Speaker: Vinh Tran This sharing session provided highly practical career orientation. The speaker analyzed the differences between an IT Helpdesk role and a System Administrator. While a Helpdesk mostly handles end-user incidents, a Sysadmin requires a mindset for governance, automation, design, and infrastructure optimization. The suggested roadmap included skill groups such as computer networking, Linux/Windows Server operating systems, scripting with Bash, PowerShell, or Python, cloud computing, and Infrastructure as Code using Terraform. The speaker also emphasized the importance of building a Home Lab to practice and accumulate real-world experience.
Key Takeaways Regarding Technical Knowledge & New Technologies Better understood containerization and the reasons why Docker is widely used in modern application deployment. Grasped how to use Dockerfiles and Docker Compose to standardize development environments. Understood how AWS WAF can combine with log streaming and Machine Learning to detect anomalous attacks. Acquired further knowledge on cloud game server architectures and requirements regarding latency and state synchronization. Understood the role of AWS Neptune in building a Graph Knowledge Base for GraphRAG. Learned how GraphRAG supports complex queries requiring multi-step reasoning. Regarding Teamwork Skills Better understood how to organize teams according to Agile/Scrum. Learned how to utilize tools like Jira, Trello, and Git to manage tasks more effectively. Recognized that clear communication and positive feedback are crucial elements in teamwork. Learned to view conflicts as opportunities for improvement rather than just negative issues. Regarding Personal Development Orientation Gained a realistic view of the career progression path from IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin. Identified the need to build a solid foundation in networking, operating systems, automation, and cloud. Understood the importance of practicing through Home Labs and learning via real-world projects. Acquired more motivation to refine skills in scripting, Linux, and Infrastructure as Code. Professional Applications Applying Docker: Use Dockerfiles and Docker Compose to package demo applications, making local execution and deployment more convenient. Improving System Security: Research further into log analysis and combining AWS WAF with anomaly detection mechanisms. Optimizing Teamwork: Apply task division, progress tracking, and clear code reviews when working in teams. Researching GraphRAG: Continue exploring AWS Neptune and data representation in graph formats to support AI use cases. Structuring a Self-Study Roadmap: Plan to study Linux Administration, Python automation, and cloud infrastructure. Overall Event Experience The event brought me a diverse range of knowledge, from application deployment foundations with Docker, security with AWS WAF, cloud game servers to GraphRAG and infrastructure career orientation. The point that impressed me was that all presentations were tied to real-world scenarios. Concepts like Docker Compose, WAF combined with Machine Learning, or Graph Knowledge Bases were not just explained theoretically but were linked to specific deployment challenges. Apart from technical knowledge, the insights on teamwork and career roadmaps also helped me clearly identify skills that need improvement during my internship.
Lessons Learned Docker is an important tool that standardizes environments and reduces deployment errors. Modern security requires a combination of rules, logs, automation, and behavioral analysis. For complex AI challenges, the way data is organized plays a very important role. Effective teamwork requires workflows, tools, and clear communication. Career development in the infrastructure field demands persistence, regular practice, and a specific learning roadmap.

title: Week 2 Worklog
content:

title: Blog 2
content:
Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture in an AWS Landing Zone: Deployment Platform for Enterprise Generative AI The Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture serves as a reference framework that enables organizations to deploy Generative AI applications securely, with robust governance, and at scale on AWS. When developing a few experimental AI applications, engineering teams can easily connect directly to Amazon Bedrock. However, as the volume of AI workloads expands, enterprises require a standardized blueprint to regulate access control, manage financial spend, protect data perimeters, and secure long-term operational efficiency.
Key Points to Master: The Imperative for a Standardized Generative AI Architecture When multiple AI workloads run simultaneously, governing access rights and monitoring resource consumption becomes highly complex. Enterprises must enforce strict data boundaries, regulate request velocity, and maintain uniform security compliance policies. A structured reference blueprint makes deploying AI across the enterprise far more manageable, auditable, and scalable. Decoupling Accounts by Functional Responsibilities Service Network Account: Dedicated to orchestrating network connectivity, managing ingestion access policies, and load balancing request traffic. Generative AI Account: Houses central Amazon Bedrock environments, foundational large language models (Foundation Models), Guardrails, and associated cognitive sub-engines. Workload Accounts: Isolated target spaces running line-of-business applications such as customer chatbots, internal research assistants, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines, or document processing engines. Regulating AI Traffic Streams via Amazon VPC Lattice Individual workload applications never establish direct point-to-point connections to Amazon Bedrock endpoints. Ingress and egress AI payload traffic streams route systematically through a centralized network proxy gateway management layer. This layout enables the enterprise to dynamically inject perimeter defense policies and trace comprehensive security access trails. Amazon Bedrock acting as a Unified AI Shared Platform Core Bedrock provides a single, controlled gateway accessing world-class Foundation Models. Enterprises can standardize the interface schemas defining how distinct application nodes consume advanced AI capabilities. Centralized lifecycle management drastically minimizes the threat of configuration drift or security misconfigurations across disparate developer teams. Reinforcing Data Boundaries using Bedrock Guardrails Guardrails dynamically evaluate, filter, and sanitize prompt inputs and model output generation blocks. This provides native protection to mitigate the exposure or leakage of sensitive corporate data fields. It directly addresses enterprise safety guidelines and stringent data compliance rules within highly regulated industries. High-Level Architecture Overview: The cross-account data processing workflow operates as follows:
Applications hosted within independent Workload Accounts request access to Generative AI engine capabilities. Instead of traversing public paths to call Amazon Bedrock endpoints directly, the workload interfaces with the centralized Service Network. Amazon VPC Lattice acts as the secure governance and routing hub, intercepting and managing the internal service traffic streams. The Generative AI Account centrally hosts, manages, and executes the core AI computational microservices. The Service Network Account coordinates interconnected channels, processes IAM security policies, and shares access across multiple organization accounts. AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) securely shares private VPC Lattice services across target AWS Accounts within the corporate AWS Organization. The Role of Deployed AWS Services: Amazon Bedrock: Serves as the core engine exposing fully managed Foundation Models to drive Generative AI calculations. Amazon VPC Lattice: Connects, isolates, and audits application-to-application traffic across complex cross-account multi-VPC networks. AWS RAM: Provisions secure resource sharing capabilities across discrete AWS Accounts inside the organization tree. Amazon Route 53: Manages internal domain name resolution (Private DNS) to ensure reliable service discovery routing across accounts. Workload Accounts: Isolates the production runtimes of specific AI consumer applications like conversational bots, RAG structures, or automated document parsers. Service Network Account: Governs the central overlay network tier and maintains absolute access control policies. Delivered Business Value: Adopting the Amazon Bedrock Baseline framework brings critical operational benefits to the enterprise:
Delivers complete, centralized governance over all corporate Generative AI endpoints and assets. Heightens data security posture and strengthens access auditing capabilities. Facilitates precise cost-allocation tracking across multiple independent line-of-business workloads. Integrates natively with the standard multi-account structures of an enterprise AWS Landing Zone setup. Secures a seamless growth path transitioning from early prototype phases up to global production-grade scale. Shifts the enterprise paradigm toward building a shared, reusable AI platform instead of allowing siloed development teams to spin up disconnected environments. Conclusion The Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture represents more than just a typical cloud networking blueprint; it is a vital strategy for governing Generative AI at an enterprise scale. By isolating cognitive computing layers into dedicated AI accounts, regulating API streams via Amazon VPC Lattice, treating Bedrock as a unified shared-service core, and enforcing centralized compliance guardrails, enterprises can roll out Generative AI securely, reliably, and efficiently. For cloud architects, this model offers invaluable architectural design lessons, demonstrating that when deploying AI in an enterprise landscape, success goes far beyond simply calling a model endpoint—it hinges on building robust governance, rigid data privacy, cost control, and long-term operational viability.
Illustrative Diagram Original Blog Reference https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/amazon-bedrock-baseline-architecture-in-an-aws-landing-zone/

title: Deploy Infrastructure with AWS CDK
content:
This step deploys the entire DMS infrastructure to AWS using a single CDK command. The CDK stack provisions all services automatically.
What Gets Deployed The DmsStack creates the following resources in order:
1. Amazon Cognito — User Pool
Pool name: dms-dev Sign-in by email only, self-registration disabled 3 user groups: EMPLOYEE (precedence 30), DEPARTMENT_ADMIN (precedence 20), SYSTEM_ADMIN (precedence 10) Password policy: minimum 12 characters, requires digits, uppercase, lowercase, and symbols Token validity: Access/ID token = 60 minutes, Refresh token = 7 days 2. Amazon S3 — Three Private Buckets FrontendBucket — hosts the compiled React SPA QuarantineBucket — receives all file uploads first; GuardDuty scans here DocumentsBucket — stores only clean, approved documents All buckets block public access; CORS configured on QuarantineBucket for PUT operations 3. Amazon DynamoDB — Single Table
Table name: dms-dev Billing: PAY_PER_REQUEST (no capacity planning needed) Encryption: AWS_MANAGED Point-In-Time Recovery (PITR): enabled TTL attribute: expiresAtEpoch (for auto-expiring UploadIntent records) 4 Global Secondary Indexes (GSI1–GSI4) for various query patterns 4. AWS Lambda → 12 functions visible (Node.js 22, ARM64) Handler Purpose me Get current user profile documents List documents for a user/department document-detail Get a single document with versions document-audit-events Get audit log for a document upload-intents Create presigned PUT URL to QuarantineBucket download-intents Create presigned GET URL from DocumentsBucket document-sharing Share/revoke document access admin-users Create/manage users in Cognito analytics Aggregate storage and activity statistics process-upload EventBridge target: move clean files, write DynamoDB process-upload-dlq Handle failed event deliveries from Dead Letter Queue 5. Amazon EventBridge — Event Routing
Event bus routing S3 ObjectCreated events directly to the processing Lambda Dead Letter Queue: handles failed event deliveries if Lambda processing fails S3 QuarantineBucket sends events to EventBridge on every s3:ObjectCreated:Put under the quarantine/ prefix 6. GuardDuty Malware Protection Plan
Dedicated IAM role grants GuardDuty access to scan QuarantineBucket Files are tagged CLEAN or THREAT_FOUND after scanning 7. Amazon API Gateway — REST API
Cognito Authorizer validates JWT on all protected routes Routes map to Lambda functions by HTTP method and path 8. Amazon CloudFront — CDN for Frontend
Serves the React SPA from FrontendBucket via CloudFront OAC 9. CloudWatch + SNS Alerts
Log groups with 2-week retention (dev) or 3-month retention (production) SNS topic sends email alerts for cost anomalies or Lambda errors Deploy Command Navigate to the infrastructure directory and run:
cd aws/infrastructure npm install npx cdk deploy -c environment=dev -c alertEmail=your-email@example.com Or use the environment variable:
DMS_ALERT_EMAIL=your-email@example.com npx cdk deploy -c environment=dev Verify Deployment After cdk deploy completes, check the Outputs section in the terminal. You should see:
UserPoolId — the Cognito User Pool ID UserPoolClientId — the Cognito App Client ID ApiUrl — the API Gateway endpoint URL CloudFrontUrl — the frontend URL You can also verify in the AWS Console:
CloudFormation → Stack DmsStack-dev → Status: CREATE_COMPLETE DynamoDB → Table dms-dev exists with 4 GSIs S3 → Three buckets created Lambda → 11 functions visible Cognito → User pool dms-dev with 3 groups

title: Event 3
content:
Event Report: “FCAJ Community Day (June 27)” Purpose of the Event The FCAJ Community Day (June 27) event was organized to introduce new technology projects, focusing particularly on AI Agents, operations automation, AI voice assistants, Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Q, and secure connectivity solutions within enterprise environments. The main objectives of the event included:
Sharing practical solutions researched and developed by members. Introducing AI application trends in system operations, DevOps, customer service, and workforce planning. Presenting methods for building automated systems that detect, analyze, and resolve incidents. Clarifying the roles of Multi-Agent Systems, Voice Agents, Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Q, and MCP in modern AI architectures. Providing practical perspectives on data security when integrating AI into enterprise systems. List of Speakers & Topics Steve Tran - Topic: Deep Response Engine: From Detection to Autonomous Resolution Nghia Danh - Trung Vu - Kiet Tran - Topic: Voice Agents: Building Human-Like AI Conversations at Scale Nguyen Nguyen - Bao Phan - Topic: AWS DevOps Agent: Your Always-Available Operations Teammate Truong Tran - Minh Anh - Topic: AI-Powered Productivity: Workforce Planning For Enterprise Toan Nguyen - Nghia Danh - Topic: Building Secure Private MCP Connection with Amazon Quick Key Highlights of the Presentations 1. Deep Response Engine: From Detection to Autonomous Resolution - Speaker: Steve Tran The presentation focused on modern cloud system operations, where the volume of alerts is increasing and the complexity of microservices architectures is continually growing. Relying solely on traditional operations models can easily overwhelm technical teams with too many alerts. The speaker introduced a new approach: shifting from Alert-driven to Action-driven operations. Instead of merely detecting and sending alerts, the system can automatically analyze root causes, propose remediation steps, and in some cases, autonomously execute corrective actions. The Autonomous Incident Response demo helped me better visualize how a system can automatically detect a service fault, trigger the remediation process, and restore operations without extensive manual intervention.
2. Voice Agents: Building Human-Like AI Conversations at Scale - Speaker: Nghia Danh - Trung Vu - Kiet Tran This topic presented the evolution from traditional IVR systems and chatbots to AI Voice Agents capable of more natural conversations. The content emphasized the challenges of building voice assistants at scale, such as response latency, accuracy, context understanding, and user experience. The speakers introduced how to utilize Amazon Bedrock and Amazon Nova Sonic to build speech-to-speech communication models. The architecture also combines components like telephony, streaming, and MCP to create a smoother conversational flow. The demo clearly demonstrated the potential of voice agents in fields such as customer service, automated call centers, internal consultation, and business operations support.
3. AWS DevOps Agent: Your Always-Available Operations Teammate - Speaker: Nguyen Nguyen - Bao Phan The presentation introduced the AWS DevOps Agent model as a virtual operations assistant that is always ready to support technical teams. Instead of engineers having to manually check logs, find errors, and troubleshoot, the agent can assist in analyzing incidents and proposing remediation actions. The content focused on reducing two critical metrics in system operations: MTTD and MTTR. When detection time and remediation time are shortened, the system becomes more stable and minimizes the impact on end-users. The speakers also presented how to use Bedrock AgentCore and multi-agent reasoning mechanisms to allow multiple agents to coordinate and handle complex problems. The demo on an Amazon ECS container environment showed that the agent can assist in detecting errors and resolving incidents within container infrastructure.
4. AI-Powered Productivity: Workforce Planning For Enterprise - Speaker: Truong Tran - Minh Anh This presentation focused on the application of AI in the field of human resources and enterprise resource planning. In large organizations, managing personnel, allocating resources, handling requests, and forecasting labor demand is a highly complex problem. The speakers introduced how Amazon Q can assist in analyzing internal data, helping HR departments shorten task processing times and make data-driven decisions. This content helped me understand that AI is not only used in technical tasks or chatbots, but can also powerfully support business functions such as human resources, operations, and corporate governance.
5. Building Secure Private MCP Connection with Amazon Quick - Speaker: Toan Nguyen - Nghia Danh The presentation addressed security challenges when AI platforms need to connect to data sources or MCP Servers. If connections traverse the public internet, enterprises may face risks of data leakage or unauthorized access. The introduced solution is establishing VPC Private Connectivity so that Amazon Quick can connect to MCP Servers more privately and securely. This content emphasized the importance of designing security from the ground up when deploying AI in enterprise environments. Through this part, I better understood the roles of private connections, network isolation, and access control when building AI systems that process sensitive data.
Key Takeaways Regarding AI Agents & Generative AI Gained more understanding of how AI Agents can assist in system operations, error analysis, and automated remediation actions. Grasped the concept of multi-agent reasoning, where multiple agents coordinate to solve a complex problem. Learned about the development direction of voice agents with Amazon Bedrock and Amazon Nova Sonic. Recognized that deploying AI in enterprises is not just about creating chatbots, but must also address security, data, workflows, and scalability. Regarding Operations & DevOps Understood the difference between alert-driven operations models and automated action-driven operations models. Grasped the importance of MTTD and MTTR metrics in evaluating operational efficiency. Acquired more perspective on how AI can support DevOps engineers in monitoring and handling incidents. Realized that operations automation can help reduce the load on technical teams and increase system stability. Regarding Cloud Security Understood the role of VPC Private Connectivity in protecting sensitive data flows. Learned how private connections help mitigate risks when integrating AI with internal data sources. Recognized that enterprise AI systems need to be designed following a Security by Design approach. Acquired further knowledge on how to build secure connections between an AI Assistant Platform and an MCP Server. Professional Applications Researching AI Agents: Learn more about how to build simple agents to support log monitoring, detect errors, or suggest incident remediation actions. Operations Automation: Apply action-driven thinking to labs or personal projects to reduce dependency on manual operations. Designing Private Connections: When building systems with sensitive data, prioritize using VPC Endpoints or private connectivity instead of letting traffic traverse the public internet. Exploring Amazon Bedrock: Continue researching AWS Generative AI services, particularly Bedrock Agents, Nova Sonic, and models serving voice interactions. Improving Security Thinking: When integrating AI into a system, consider access permissions, data location, connection flows, and auditing mechanisms right from the design phase. Overall Event Experience The event brought me many new perspectives on how AI is being implemented into actual enterprise operations. The presentations did not just talk about AI at an conceptual level, but focused on specific issues such as incident handling, workforce optimization, building voice agents, and protecting data connections. The live demos were what impressed me the most. Thanks to the demos, I could easily visualize how an AI Agent or a self-healing system could operate in a real-world situation. The topics were also arranged logically, moving from system operations, voice agents, DevOps agents, workforce planning, to secure private connections. In addition to technical knowledge, the event helped me realize that AI in enterprises must be accompanied by governance, security, and clear operational workflows. These are crucial factors if one wants to deploy AI at scale.
Lessons Learned AI Agents can become an important part of modern system operations. Automation not only saves time but also helps reduce errors caused by manual operations. When deploying AI in enterprises, security and data control must be placed first. Metrics such as MTTD and MTTR are very important when evaluating system operations quality. Private connectivity is the preferred choice when connecting AI to internal systems or data. Generative AI will deliver greater value when integrated into the correct real-world business workflows. Some Photos from the Event

title: Blog 3
content:
Emailing Daily Delta Cost Reports in a Multi-Account Environment Using AWS Lambda and Cost Explorer In an AWS multi-account environment, tracking costs is an important issue but not always easy to implement. In particular, the finance team or management often do not have direct access to the AWS Billing Console, making cost information sharing a manual process. This article introduces an automated solution to send daily delta cost reports using AWS Lambda, AWS Cost Explorer API, Amazon EventBridge, and Amazon Simple Email Service (SES). This is a serverless architecture that increases cost visibility and supports more effective cloud management.
Key Points to Master: The cost management problem in a multi-account environment Enterprises usually have multiple AWS accounts such as Production, Non-Production, and Sandbox. Billing information is usually centralized in the management account. Not every finance member or leadership has access to the AWS Billing Console. Summarizing and sending cost reports manually is time-consuming and lacks timeliness. The concept of delta cost Delta cost is the change in cost between the current day and the previous day. This metric helps quickly detect anomalous fluctuations. When costs increase suddenly, the team can check the cause earlier. This is a simple way to increase cost visibility within the organization. Automating reports with Lambda Lambda is triggered on a daily schedule. The function calls the AWS Cost Explorer API to get cost data. Data is processed, percentage changes are calculated, and formatted into an HTML report. The report is sent via email to the recipient list. Sending emails with Amazon SES Amazon SES is used to send cost reports to finance, leadership, or stakeholders. Recipients do not need to log in to the AWS Console to view the cost situation. Automated sending helps reduce manual operations in the management process. Allying with FinOps direction The solution helps the technical team and finance team track costs together more easily. Increases transparency in cloud resource utilization. Supports early detection of accounts or environments with abnormally increasing costs. High-Level Architecture Overview: The processing flow can be simply understood as follows:
Amazon EventBridge triggers Lambda according to a daily cron schedule. AWS Lambda calls the AWS Cost Explorer API to get costs from accounts in the organization. Lambda calculates the cost difference compared to the previous day. Lambda formats the data into an HTML report. Amazon SES sends the report to the recipient list. Recipients can track daily costs without accessing the Billing Console. The Role of Deployed AWS Services: Amazon EventBridge: Triggers Lambda automatically on a daily schedule. AWS Lambda: Processes the logic of getting cost data, calculating delta, and creating reports. AWS Cost Explorer API: Provides cost and usage data of AWS accounts. Amazon SES: Sends cost reports via email. AWS Organizations: Allows managing multiple AWS accounts in the same organization. IAM Role: Grants permissions for Lambda to access Cost Explorer and send emails. Primary Deployment Checklist: Download the CloudFormation template from the original article. Configure the list of AWS accounts to track. Configure the list of recipient emails. Verify emails in Amazon SES. Deploy the CloudFormation stack. Grant appropriate Billing permissions to the IAM Role. Configure the EventBridge rule with a cron expression. Check the automatically sent cost report email. Delivered Business Value: This solution brings many practical benefits to the enterprise:
Automates the cost report sending process. Helps the finance team and leadership track costs more easily. Increases cost visibility in a multi-account environment. Early detects abnormal cost fluctuations. Reduces manual operations when summarizing reports. Can be extended to store reports in S3 or build long-term analysis dashboards. Conclusion The solution of sending daily delta cost reports using AWS Lambda and Cost Explorer is a practical example of using serverless to automate cloud operations. The point I find interesting is that this architecture is quite neat, using managed services of AWS such as Lambda, EventBridge, Cost Explorer API, and SES. Thanks to that, enterprises can track costs more proactively without granting Billing Console access to too many people. For me, this is a pattern very worth learning because it combines automation, cost management, and FinOps in an AWS multi-account environment.
Illustrative Diagram Original Blog Reference https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/architecture/email-delta-cost-usage-report-in-a-multi-account-organization-using-aws-lambda/

title: Configure Backend & Test APIs
content:
After the infrastructure is deployed, this step walks through creating the first user, uploading a document, and verifying the full end-to-end flow.
1. Create the First Admin User Since selfSignUpEnabled: false, all users must be created by an administrator.
Go to AWS Console → Cognito → User Pools → dms-dev → Users → Create user:
Email: admin@yourcompany.com Temporary password: Set a strong one (will be changed on first login) After creation, go to Groups tab and add the user to SYSTEM_ADMIN Or use the AWS CLI:
aws cognito-idp admin-create-user \ --user-pool-id <UserPoolId> \ --username admin@yourcompany.com \ --temporary-password "Temp@12345!" \ --user-attributes Name=email,Value=admin@yourcompany.com Name=email_verified,Value=true Name=name,Value="Admin User" aws cognito-idp admin-add-user-to-group \ --user-pool-id <UserPoolId> \ --username admin@yourcompany.com \ --group-name SYSTEM_ADMIN 2. Authenticate and Get JWT Token Use the Cognito USER_PASSWORD_AUTH flow to get an access token:
aws cognito-idp initiate-auth \ --auth-flow USER_PASSWORD_AUTH \ --client-id <UserPoolClientId> \ --auth-parameters USERNAME=admin@yourcompany.com,PASSWORD="YourNewPassword" Copy the AccessToken from the response. You will use this as a Bearer token in all API calls.
3. Test the /me Endpoint curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <AccessToken>" \ https://<ApiUrl>/me Expected response:
{ "userId": "...", "email": "admin@yourcompany.com", "name": "Admin User", "groups": ["SYSTEM_ADMIN"] } 4. Upload a Document (Full Flow) Step 4a — Request a Presigned Upload URL:
curl -X POST \ -H "Authorization: Bearer <AccessToken>" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"filename": "report.pdf", "contentType": "application/pdf", "size": 102400}' \ https://<ApiUrl>/upload-intents Response includes:
{ "uploadIntentId": "ui-xxxx", "presignedUrl": "https://s3.amazonaws.com/quarantine-bucket/quarantine/...", "expiresAt": "2026-07-03T14:00:00Z" } Step 4b — Upload directly to S3 using the Presigned URL:
curl -X PUT \ -H "Content-Type: application/pdf" \ --data-binary @report.pdf \ "<presignedUrl>" Step 4c — Verify the pipeline ran automatically:
Go to S3 → QuarantineBucket → verify the file appeared Go to GuardDuty → Malware Protection → verify the scan ran and tagged the file Go to S3 → DocumentsBucket → the clean file should appear here after ~30 seconds Go to DynamoDB → dms-dev → check for a new DOC#... record 5. List Documents curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <AccessToken>" \ https://<ApiUrl>/documents You should see the uploaded document in the response with its documentId, filename, versionNumber, and timestamps.
6. Run the Frontend Locally cd frontend npm install npm run dev Open http://localhost:5173 in your browser. Enter the UserPoolId, UserPoolClientId, and ApiUrl from the CDK outputs to configure the app, then log in with your admin credentials.
7. Explore Frontend Features Once logged in, you can test various features provided by the React SPA:
Download Document: Share Document: Manage Users (System Admin only): Department Management (Department Admin only): Audit Logs & Admin History: Analytics Dashboard:

Friendly AI Assistant (Hai Anh): Introduced how to build user-friendly chatbots capable of supporting data analysis and visualization on the AWS platform. From Edge to Origin (Thinh Nguyen): Presented methods for optimizing global content delivery using Amazon CloudFront, helping to reduce latency, accelerate access speeds, and safeguard origin servers. 36 hrs with LotusHacks (Team VIB): Brought a practical perspective on teamwork within a Hackathon, spanning from the ideation phase to the continuous 36-hour development and demo of the UTMorpho product. How LLM actually works? (Dao Duc): Helped attendees better understand large language models, particularly the Transformer architecture, Self-Attention mechanism, Pre-training, and Fine-tuning. title: Security & Monitoring
content:
The DMS system applies multiple layers of security and monitoring to protect data and detect anomalies in real-time.
1. IAM — Principle of Least Privilege Each Lambda function has its own dedicated IAM role with only the permissions it needs:
Lambda Permissions Granted documents dynamodb:Query on Documents table (read-only) upload-intents dynamodb:PutItem + s3:PutObject on QuarantineBucket only download-intents dynamodb:GetItem + s3:GetObject on DocumentsBucket only admin-users cognito-idp:AdminCreateUser, AdminAddUserToGroup, etc. process-upload s3:GetObject on Quarantine + s3:PutObject on Documents + dynamodb:PutItem No Lambda function has * permissions or cross-service overprivileged access.
2. S3 — Private Buckets & Presigned URLs All three S3 buckets have Block All Public Access enabled. Documents are never directly accessible via public URLs. Access is granted only through time-limited Presigned URLs generated by Lambda: Upload: Presigned PUT URL (expires in ~15 minutes) Download: Presigned GET URL (expires in ~5 minutes) S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3) is applied to all stored objects. 3. GuardDuty — Malware Protection The MalwareProtectionPlan resource scans every file uploaded to QuarantineBucket:
File arrives in quarantine/ prefix via Presigned URL. GuardDuty automatically scans the file. GuardDuty tags the S3 object: GuardDutyMalwareScanStatus: CLEAN → safe to process GuardDutyMalwareScanStatus: THREAT_FOUND → discard, log to audit Lambda process-upload reads the tag before moving the file. To view scan results:
Go to GuardDuty → Malware Protection → Protected buckets Or check the S3 object tags directly in the Console 4. Cognito — Role-Based Access Control Three user groups enforce different permission levels in the backend:
Group Access Level EMPLOYEE (precedence 30) Can upload, view, and download their own documents DEPARTMENT_ADMIN (precedence 20) Can manage documents for their department, share with users SYSTEM_ADMIN (precedence 10) Full access — manage users, view analytics, access all documents The group claim is embedded in the JWT token and validated by Lambda at runtime.
5. CloudWatch — Log Monitoring All 11 Lambda functions write structured logs to CloudWatch Log Groups:
Log retention: 2 weeks for dev, 3 months for production X-Ray tracing is enabled on all Lambda functions for end-to-end request tracing To view logs:
Go to CloudWatch → Log Groups Find /aws/lambda/DmsStack-dev-<FunctionName> Use Insights to query: fields @timestamp, @message | sort @timestamp desc | limit 50 6. SNS — Cost & Error Alerts A CloudWatch Alarm is connected to an SNS topic that emails your alertEmail when:
Lambda error rate exceeds a threshold Monthly estimated cost goes above budget To verify alerts are working:
Check SNS → Topics → DmsAlertTopic Confirm your email subscription is Confirmed Check CloudWatch → Alarms to see active alarm definitions

title: Workshop
content:
Document Management System (DMS) on AWS Serverless Overview The Document Management System (DMS) is a cloud-native application built entirely on the AWS Serverless ecosystem. It allows internal users to securely upload, version, share, and audit company documents — without managing any servers.
The system is deployed via AWS CDK (Infrastructure as Code) and integrates the following core AWS services:
Amazon Cognito — User authentication and role-based access control (EMPLOYEE / DEPARTMENT_ADMIN / SYSTEM_ADMIN) Amazon API Gateway — Single REST API entry point with Cognito Authorizer AWS Lambda — Serverless business logic handlers (Node.js 22, ARM64) Amazon S3 — Private document storage (DocumentsBucket) and upload quarantine (QuarantineBucket) Amazon DynamoDB — Single-table design with GSI indexes for metadata, versioning, sharing, and audit logs Amazon EventBridge — Serverless event bus routing S3 events to Lambda Amazon GuardDuty — Malware Protection scanning for uploaded files Amazon CloudFront — CDN delivery for the React frontend Amazon CloudWatch — Centralized log monitoring and cost alerting Amazon SNS — Email alerts for anomalous cost or system errors Architecture The data flow follows this pattern:
The user logs in via the React frontend using Cognito and receives a JWT token. All API calls go through API Gateway, which validates the JWT via Cognito Authorizer. File uploads use Presigned URLs — the frontend uploads directly to QuarantineBucket (S3), bypassing API Gateway. Amazon EventBridge routes an Object Created event to Lambda when a new file lands in the quarantine bucket. GuardDuty Malware Protection scans the file and tags it with CLEAN or THREAT_FOUND. A Lambda worker triggered by EventBridge reads the GuardDuty scan tag, moves clean files to DocumentsBucket, and writes metadata + version records to DynamoDB. Document sharing, audit logs, analytics, and user management are all handled by dedicated Lambda functions. Workshop Content Workshop Overview Prerequisites Deploy Infrastructure with AWS CDK Configure Backend & Test APIs Security & Monitoring with GuardDuty and CloudWatch Clean Up Resources

title: Clean Up Resources
content:
When you are done with the workshop, follow these steps to delete all AWS resources and avoid ongoing charges.
1. Destroy the CDK Stack Run the following command from the aws/infrastructure directory:
cd aws/infrastructure npx cdk destroy -c environment=dev CDK will ask for confirmation:
Are you sure you want to delete: DmsStack-dev (y/n)? y This command will automatically delete:
All 3 S3 buckets (FrontendBucket, QuarantineBucket, DocumentsBucket) and their contents DynamoDB table dms-dev and all data All 12 Lambda functions and their CloudWatch Log Groups API Gateway REST API Cognito User Pool and all users EventBridge Rules and targets CloudFront distribution SNS topic and email subscriptions GuardDuty Malware Protection Plan and IAM role 2. Verify Deletion in AWS Console After the destroy command completes, verify that resources have been removed:
CloudFormation → confirm DmsStack-dev stack no longer exists S3 → confirm the 3 DMS buckets are gone DynamoDB → confirm table dms-dev is deleted Lambda → confirm DMS functions are removed Cognito → confirm user pool dms-dev is deleted 3. Remove CDK Bootstrap (Optional) If you want to fully clean up and remove the CDK bootstrap stack as well:
aws cloudformation delete-stack --stack-name CDKToolkit 4. Check for Remaining Charges After cleanup, verify in AWS Billing → Cost Explorer that no DMS-related charges appear in the next billing cycle. Common items to check:
S3 storage charges DynamoDB read/write units Lambda invocations CloudWatch log storage GuardDuty scan charges Summary Congratulations on completing the DMS Workshop! You have successfully:
Deployed a fully serverless Document Management System on AWS using CDK Configured role-based access control with Amazon Cognito Implemented a secure upload pipeline with S3 Presigned URLs and GuardDuty malware scanning Used a single-table DynamoDB design with 4 GSIs for efficient querying Set up CloudWatch logging, X-Ray tracing, and SNS cost alerts Verified the full end-to-end document upload, scan, and retrieval flow

title: Feedback and Suggestions
content:
Overall Evaluation 1. Work Environment
During my internship, I felt that the working environment was very friendly, open, and highly supportive. The mentors, admin team, and fellow members in the program were always ready to guide me whenever I faced difficulties. This made me feel comfortable asking questions, discussing issues, and proactively learning new knowledge.
The professional workspace, excellent sharing spirit, and organized learning activities also gave me additional motivation throughout the internship process.
2. Support from Mentors / Admin Team
The mentors were dedicated, explaining issues clearly when I did not understand and always encouraging me to research independently before giving answers. This helped me practice my self-learning abilities and problem-solving mindset without depending too much on instructors.
The admin team also provided great support in offering information, materials, schedules, and necessary guidelines. Thanks to this, I could closely follow the internship progress and complete the assigned tasks more smoothly.
3. Alignment between Work and Major
The internship content is highly suitable for my learning orientation and career development goals.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities
The internship gave me numerous opportunities to develop both my technical and soft skills. Technically, I trained my abilities in reading technical documentation, analyzing architectures, writing reports, building worklogs, and exploring AWS services topic by topic.
In addition, I improved my time management, teamwork, content presentation, and knowledge synthesis skills. Having to conduct independent research, take personal notes, and systematically organize knowledge helped me become more proactive in my learning process.
5. Culture & Team Spirit
I highly appreciate the supportive and sharing spirit within the internship program. Everyone worked seriously while maintaining a cheerful, approachable, and respectful atmosphere. When issues arose, members were always willing to discuss them to find solutions together.
6. Policies / Benefits for Interns
The internship program was well-prepared with clear content, schedules, and learning directions. The workshops, tech sharing sessions, and demo days allowed me to approach many new topics, extending beyond AWS to AI, DevOps, security, teamwork, and career orientation.
I consider this a very positive aspect because interns do not just write reports but also have the opportunity to learn from many different perspectives.
